runs.interpretation: Global labor-supply substitution-response elasticity override used in a U.S. microsimulation model, representing the percentage change in labor supply for each 1 percent change in the effective marginal wage, applied uniformly across individuals.

runs.reasoningSummary: Labor-supply substitution elasticities in the literature typically range from near-zero to around 0.5 for broad population averages, with considerable variation by demographic group. For a uniform global parameter in a tax-benefit microsimulation applied to all U.S. adults, a moderate value in the lower-to-middle range is most defensible, as it balances the evidence that intensive-margin elasticities for primary earners are modest (0.1–0.3) while recognizing some heterogeneity. A point estimate of 0.25 reflects a conventionally cited midpoint, with wide uncertainty around it.

Uncertainty around a central 0.25 estimate is substantial given heterogeneity in methodology and population definition.

runs.reasoningSummary: Labor supply substitution elasticity estimates vary substantially by population, specification, and estimation method. For a uniform elasticity applied across all U.S. adults in a microsimulation, I center on 0.25 as a moderate estimate that reflects empirical studies on broad populations—higher than pure compensated elasticity for some groups but acknowledging heterogeneity across workers. runs.reasoningSummary: Labor-supply substitution elasticity estimates in the literature typically range from near-zero to around 0.5–0.8 for wage elasticities, with substantial heterogeneity by demographic group.
